metadata.summary: A framework combining predictive (waterfall) and agile delivery when neither alone fits. Define boundaries explicitly, use a single work-graph, and maintain a translation layer between vocabularies.

content.title: Hybrid Delivery: Combining Predictive and Agile Approaches
content.summary: Hybrid delivery is a framework for programs that combine predictive (waterfall) and agile delivery modes when neither alone fits. The boundary between modes is defined explicitly in a program.yaml work-graph: top-level milestones (predictive arm) decompose into epics, which decompose into team backlogs (agile arm). Both arms use their own vocabulary and rituals; a translation layer converts between them for steering-committee reporting.

why.p: Pure agile or pure waterfall rarely fits real programs. Hardware + software combinations, regulated environments, and enterprise rollouts all have components with different risk profiles and cadence needs. Hybrid works when the boundary is explicit; it fails — degenerating into incoherence — when the boundary is undefined or driven by politics rather than risk profile.

The translation layer prevents vocabulary collisions from breaking executive trust. Milestone-vs-increment language, effort-based-vs-point-based estimation, and stage-gate-vs-iteration cadence all require explicit mapping. Without translation, steering committees lose confidence and programs revert to single-mode orthodoxy.

list.item: Programs with hardware + software components where physical-world milestones are predictive and digital iteration is agile.
Regulated software (FDA, FAA, ISO 26262, SOX, GDPR) needing stage gates on top of agile execution.
Enterprise transformation rollouts: portfolio-level milestones and budget cycles with Scrum/Kanban delivery teams.
Vendor + internal team mixes where vendor contracts are fixed-bid and internal teams iterate.
DevOps + Agile delivery with monthly/quarterly ops/finance/security governance reviews.
Pure software-only product teams with autonomous backlog and no compliance gates — full Scrum or Kanban is simpler.
Tiny teams (under 10) where ceremony overhead exceeds coordination value — pick one mode.
Pure fixed-scope delivery where iteration adds risk without value — stay predictive.
"We do hybrid" without explicit boundaries — that is incoherence, not hybrid; refuse and force a real method choice.

section.name: decision-framework
section.p: Hybrid delivery starts with explicit risk-profile-based mode selection. Each deliverable must be assigned clearly to one mode: predictive, agile, or a hybrid pattern.

rule.statement: Assign each deliverable explicitly to one mode: high uncertainty + frequent change = agile; clear requirements + fixed scope = predictive; mixed characteristics = hybrid; continuous deployment needed = agile + DevOps. Document the assignment in program.yaml.
rule.rationale: Mode selection by team preference or political convenience — rather than risk profile — is the primary cause of hybrid program failure.

section.name: five-hybrid-patterns
section.p: Five canonical patterns have emerged in practice, each suited to different risk profiles and organizational contexts.

list.item: Water-Scrum-Fall: waterfall for requirements and design, Scrum for development and testing. Use for hardware + software programs where design lock-in is required before code.
Agile with Gates: agile execution inside stage-gate checkpoints. Use for regulated industries (FDA, SOX, HIPAA) needing evidence at governance boundaries.
Phased Agile: multiple teams with milestone-gated phases. Use for large enterprise rollouts where each phase has a hard commitment date but internal delivery is agile.
Selective Agile: some components agile, others predictive by risk profile. Use when a single program spans multiple delivery domains (hardware predictive, firmware agile, cloud-native DevOps).
DevOps + Agile: continuous delivery with governance reviews. 2026 default for cloud-native programs; ship on demand, review finance/security monthly or quarterly.

section.name: explicit-boundary-rule
section.p: The most critical rule in hybrid delivery: the boundary between predictive and agile must be drawn explicitly and visible to every participant.

rule.statement: Name in writing which deliverables are predictive (charter, contracts, regulatory submissions, hardware BOMs) and which are agile (software increments, user research, internal services). If you cannot draw a literal line on an architecture or organizational diagram, the model is incoherent — do not proceed.
rule.rationale: Boundary ambiguity is the dominant hybrid failure mode. Without an explicit line, every gate review becomes a negotiation about which rules apply, and the program devolves into political theatre.

rule.statement: Maintain one canonical work-graph (program.yaml) with milestones, epics, and team mappings. Two competing portfolios — one for governance, one for delivery — creates divergent truth that agents and humans both get wrong.
rule.rationale: Tool sprawl (backlog in Jira, gantt in MS Project, deliverables in SharePoint) means agents spend more tokens reconciling than executing. One system of record prevents drift.

section.name: tool-count-constraint
section.p: Each additional tool introduces a reconciliation step. The number of tools should be proportional to the complexity of the boundary.

rule.statement: Choose a tool count proportional to boundary count: ideally one tool spans both arms (Azure DevOps, GitLab Ultimate, Jira+Plans). Two tools maximum with an explicit sync mechanism. Three or more tools is a smell indicating an unresolved process problem.
rule.rationale: Each additional tool adds a reconciliation step; three+ tools guarantee drift between arms that becomes visible only at gate reviews, when correction is most expensive.

section.name: translation-table
section.p: Agile and predictive vocabularies are fundamentally different. A translation layer converts between them for steering-committee reporting and cross-functional coordination.

rule.statement: Define one explicit translation table before the first steering report: epic maps to deliverable, story-points map to a declared effort estimate, velocity maps to run-rate. Use the same table for every report cycle and never mix vocabularies within a single audience.
rule.rationale: Vocabulary leakage — "milestone" used in team standups, "increment" in governance packs — causes executives to lose confidence in the program; consistent translation maintains trust.

example.title: Translation table example
example.code:
Steering-committee pack uses: milestone, deliverable, planned-vs-actual labour, variance. Team ceremony uses: increment, story, velocity, sprint goal.
Translation: - Epic (agile) ↔ Deliverable (predictive) - Story points (agile) ↔ Effort estimate in hours (predictive) - Velocity trend (agile) ↔ Run-rate labour cost (predictive) - Iteration (agile) ↔ Phase gate (predictive)
The cadence-translator-agent converts between them; humans never mix the two.

antipattern.description: Agile metrics (story points, velocity) presented directly to a steering committee that thinks in deliverables and labour cost.
antipattern.reason: Executive audiences interpret story points as arbitrary numbers; without translation they discount the data or demand a switch to hours, undermining agile capacity planning.

section.name: gate-cadence
section.p: Gate frequency must match the risk profile of the predictive arm. Too frequent gates throttle delivery; too infrequent gates miss real exposure.

rule.statement: Set gate frequency proportional to risk: monthly for regulated programs, quarterly for steady-state, never weekly (that is just a status meeting renamed). Gate decisions — go/no-go — are sponsor calls only; agents may flag readiness but not declare it.
rule.rationale: Weekly gates produce decision fatigue and get rubber-stamped. Monthly gates align with regulatory review cadence. Quarterly gates suit established, low-risk steady-state programs.

rule.statement: Re-baseline the predictive arm only when scope or contract changes, not when velocity slips. Velocity slippage is handled in retrospectives and planning; it is not a baseline trigger.
rule.rationale: Re-baselining on velocity drift obscures the original commitment and makes trend analysis impossible; the baseline is the contract, not the forecast.

section.name: evm-on-agile-arm-warning
section.p: Earned Value Management is a powerful predictive-arm tool, but it is misapplied to the agile arm with high frequency and dangerous consequences.

antipattern.description: Applying Earned Value Management metrics (CPI, SPI computed from story-points × hourly rate) to the agile arm without an explicitly agreed conversion.
antipattern.reason: Story points are a relative planning unit, not a labour-cost proxy; EVM computed from them produces numbers that look precise but are meaningless, and finance will hold the PM accountable for them.

section.name: organizational-anti-patterns
section.p: Hybrid program failure is often organizational rather than methodological. Three anti-patterns dominate.

antipattern.description: Hybrid chosen as a political compromise rather than a risk-profile decision.
antipattern.reason: PMO-wants-control + teams-want-autonomy resolved by "we do hybrid" produces neither the governance of predictive nor the adaptability of agile — it produces incoherence dressed as methodology.

antipattern.description: Agile coach distrusts gantts; PM distrusts standups.
antipattern.reason: The dominant cultural failure in hybrid programs. The solution is cross-training: PMs shadow stand-ups, coaches attend gate reviews. Neither arm is optional.

antipattern.description: Governance drift: the predictive arm enforces a stage gate, the agile arm flies through it; or vice versa, agile is gated to a quarterly cadence and dies.
antipattern.reason: Gate frequency must match risk profile, not legacy habit or org politics. Misalignment between arms destroys the coherence that hybrid promises.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Hybrid delivery at scale requires multiple specialized subagents to maintain the work-graph, generate governance reports, and flag alignment issues.

Model the program as a single work-graph (program.yaml): top-level milestones (predictive arm) decompose into epics, epics into team backlogs (agile arm). A subagent maintains the graph, flags inconsistencies (e.g., epic past its milestone date with less than 50% issues done), and drafts gate-review packs from the underlying issue tracker. The "translation layer" is owned by a custom agent that keeps stage-gate vocabulary (deliverable, baseline, variance) reconciled with agile vocabulary (story, increment, velocity). Never let the agent unilaterally close a stage gate — gate decisions are sponsor calls.

list.item: program-graph-curator-agent (sonnet): maintains program.yaml, syncs to Jira/Linear/MS Project, computes milestone-vs-epic-vs-issue alignment.
gate-review-pack-agent (sonnet): one week before each gate, assembles deliverables list, evidence links, risk register slice, EVM snapshot, and produces a draft pack PR.
cadence-translator-agent (haiku): converts agile cycle reports into predictive-arm vocabulary for steering-committee consumption.
dual-rhythm-coach-agent (opus): activated when team velocity diverges greater than 25% from milestone burn; produces options memo (re-baseline, drop scope, add capacity, change cadence).
password-scrubber-agent: runs over gate-review packs before distribution; predictive-arm packs frequently leak credentials in screenshots.

section.name: program-graph-curator-prompt
section.p: The program-graph-curator validates the work-graph structure and emits alignment status for each milestone.

Translation prompt: "Convert this cycle report into steering-committee predictive-arm vocabulary. Map: story → deliverable, increment → milestone progress, velocity → planned-vs-actual labour. Preserve dates and quantities exactly. Do not editorialize."

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Hybrid program execution requires tools to manage both the predictive and agile arms, and to render governance artifacts.

list.item: git + tags — tag releases at gate decisions; git history = audit trail of gate outcomes.
gh / glab — drive epic-level boards (predictive view) and issue-level boards (agile view).
jira-cli — JQL queries to pull cross-team status into program graph.
python-pptx / pandoc — render gate-review packs from markdown into the format the steering committee will accept.
mermaid-cli (mmdc) — render gantts + dependency diagrams from program.yaml.
ganttproject-cli / taskjuggler — compute critical path and forecast on the predictive arm.
mpxj (Java) — read/write MS Project .mpp files for organizations that mandate them.
dvc / lakeFS — version control for non-code deliverables (CAD, datasets) on the predictive arm.
pre-commit — block edits to program.yaml that change milestone dates without rationale.
yq / jq — read/patch the program graph and JSON exports.

section.name: tool-options
section.p: Many platforms support hybrid delivery, each with different strengths for predictive vs. agile arms.

list.item: Jira (+ Advanced Roadmaps / Plans) — common hybrid host: epics+milestones in Plans, issues in team boards.
Microsoft Project + Project for the Web — enterprise gantt + portfolio; predictive-arm system of truth. Pair with Azure DevOps for agile arm.
Azure DevOps — native hybrid: portfolio Plans, team Boards, Pipelines for DevOps arm.
GitLab Premium/Ultimate (Roadmaps + Boards + Iterations) — single-pane hybrid alternative; agent-friendly via GraphQL.
Smartsheet — portfolio gantts + dashboards for organizations already on Smartsheet.
Asana (Portfolios) — portfolio-level milestones with team-level boards underneath.
ClickUp — all-in-one with goals + sprints; reporting depth limited at scale.
Monday.com — portfolio + agile boards; weak EVM.
ServiceNow SPM (formerly ITBM) — heavy enterprise PPM; required where governance is the constraint.
Planview / Clarity — portfolio + EVM-grade tooling; expensive; agent integration via REST.
Confluence / Notion — charters, gate-review packs, decision records — pair with the work-graph host.
OpenProject / Redmine / Tuleap — self-hosted hybrid hosts for regulated/airgapped organizations.
Targetprocess (Apptio) — SAFe-aligned hybrid PPM.
Hansoft (Perforce) — game/aerospace heritage; backlog + gantt in one.

Draw the boundary explicitly: name in writing which deliverables are predictive (charter, contracts, regulatory submissions, hardware BOMs) and which are agile (software increments, user research, internal services). Boundary in program.yaml.
Single backlog of epics, multiple team backlogs of stories — never two competing portfolios. Reconcile in code, not in slides.
Make the gate cadence proportional to risk — monthly for regulated, quarterly for steady-state, never weekly (that is just a status meeting).
Define one "translation table" (epic ↔ deliverable, story-points ↔ effort estimate, velocity ↔ run rate) and use it for every steering report.
Run agile teams on agile rituals, predictive workstreams on stage gates — do not force standups onto regulatory submissions or gantt charts onto user-research.
DevOps + Agile arm: pair with cicd-engineer/ and infrastructure-engineer/ skills; ship on demand, do not let governance throttle deployment frequency.
Train both PMs and Scrum Masters on the other side; the dominant cultural failure is "agile coach distrusts gantts" or "PM distrusts standups".
Budget for change-management explicitly — hybrid programs fail more on organization change than on method.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Hybrid delivery exposes specific pitfalls for automated execution. These are empirically documented across agentic hybrid deployments.

list.item: Agents over-summarize hybrid programs as "behind" because they conflate vocabulary; force the agent to report agile metrics in agile units and predictive metrics in predictive units.
LLMs auto-translate epics into "deliverables" and lose information (acceptance criteria collapse to one line). Keep both views; never destroy the agile detail.
Date arithmetic is unreliable across calendar conventions (work days vs. calendar days, fiscal vs. ISO weeks). Hard-code the convention in the prompt and have a tool, not the LLM, compute deltas.
Stage-gate "go / no-go" decisions must never be auto-emitted — flag readiness only, leave the call to the sponsor.
EVM on agile arm is a trap: agents will earnestly compute SPI/CPI from story points × hourly rate. Refuse unless the conversion is explicitly defined and accepted by finance.
Cross-tool sync drift: if Jira and MS Project disagree, the agent will pick the latest; pick the system of record per field instead and pin it in program.yaml.
Vocabulary leakage: predictive arm uses "milestone", "deliverable", "baseline"; agile arm uses "increment", "story", "velocity". Force terminology per audience or executives lose trust.
Bulk gate-pack generation is a leak risk — every pack often pulls from confidential decks/screenshots. Run password-scrubber-agent and a manual review before sharing.
Long-context drift: program graphs greater than 50 epics blow context. Page by milestone or workstream and operate on slices.
Mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoints: boundary changes, gate decisions, re-baselining, cadence changes for any team, vendor escalations.

see-also.link: PMI PMBOK 7e — Development Approach and Life Cycle Performance Domain
PMI Agile Practice Guide — hybrid life cycle patterns
SAFe — Portfolio + Large Solution + Essential layers as a hybrid blueprint
Disciplined Agile Delivery (DAD) — explicit hybrid life cycle selection
